coredata.dc:title: Reduced state carbon dots as both reductant and stabilizer for the synthesis of gold nanoparticles
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiliang
Long, Yijuan
Wang, Qinlong
Zhang, Haijie
Huang, Xiaoxiao
Zhu, Rui
Teng, Ping
Liang, Liping
Zheng, Huzhi
coredata.dc:description: The reduced state carbon dot (r-CD) is a new kind of carbon dot (CD) prepared by using sodium borohydride. Herein, we find that the r-CDs can directly reduce chloroauric acid to form gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) without adding other reducers or stabilizers. At the same time, we deduced that the hydroxyl groups (–OH) on the surfaces of r-CDs can act as both reducer and stabilizer for the synthesis of AuNPs. The AuNPs possess intrinsic catalytic activity, but compared with the AuNPs synthesized by citrate reduction, the AuNPs we prepared exhibit less excellent catalytic activity in the reduction of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) with sodium borohydride. Conversely, for catalyzing oxidation of the substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by H2O2, the AuNPs we prepared show much higher catalytic activity than the AuNPs synthesized by citrate reduction. This work may open up a new route for the applications of CDs.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of AuNPs with the r-CDs
Influence of r-CDs and HAuCl4 concentration on AuNPs synthesis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption spectra of AuNPs synthesized from different concentration of HAuCl4 and r-CDs (C-Au-1, C-Au-2, C-Au-3: the concentration of r-CDs is 1.2×10−1 mg/mL, the concentration of HAuCl4 is 1.0×10−4, 2.0×10−4 or 4.0×10−4 M, respectively; C-Au-4, C-Au-5: the concentration of r-CDs is 6.0×10−2 mg/mL, the concentration of HAuCl4 is 1.0×10−4, 2.0×10−4 M respectively. Incubating temperature: 40°C, incubating time: 24h).

(a) Absorption spectra of AuNPs synthesized at different temperature; (b) normalized absorption spectra of AuNPs synthesized at different temperature. (The concentration of r-CDs is 6.0×10−2 mg/mL, the concentration of HAuCl4 is 1.0×10−4 M, incubating time: 24h.)

TEM and size distribution of AuNPs synthesized at different temperature (a, b: 40°C; c, d: 60°C; e, f: 80°C; g, h: 100°C). The concentration of r-CDs is 6.0×10−2 mg/mL, the concentration of HAuCl4 is 1.0×10−4 M, incubating time: 24h.

(a) Absorption spectra of AuNPs synthesized at different incubating time; (b) the relationship of intensity at maximum absorption wavelength against increasing incubation time. (The concentration of r-CDs is 6.0×10−2 mg/mL, the concentration of HAuCl4 is 1.0×10−4 M, incubating temperature: 60°C.)

Plots of the concentration of 4-NP versus time for the reduction of 4-NP with NaBH4 catalyzed by different kinds of AuNPs (1: AuNPs prepared by sodium citrate, 2: AuNPs prepared by r-CDs). The concentration of AuNPs prepared by sodium citrate and AuNPs prepared by r-CDs is 5.2×10−11 M, the concentration of NaBH4 is 7.5×10−3 M, the concentration of 4-NP is 1.0×10−4 M, the time between consecutive curves is 2 (before 30min) or 6 (after 30min) min.

Kinetic curves for the reduction of 4-NP catalyzed by AuNPs prepared by sodium citrate (1) and AuNPs prepared by r-CDs (2), respectively. Ct and C0 are 4-NP concentrations at time t and 0, respectively. The concentration of AuNPs prepared by sodium citrate and AuNPs prepared by r-CDs is 5.2×10−11 M, the concentration of NaBH4 is 7.5×10−3 M, the concentration of 4-NP is 1.0×10−4 M, the time between consecutive curves is 2 (before 30min) or 6 (after 30min) min.

Plots of the concentration of oxidation products of TMB versus time for the oxidation of TMB with H2O2 catalyzed by different kinds of AuNPs or CDs (1: AuNPs prepared by sodium citrate, 2: AuNPs prepared by r-CDs, 3: r-CDs). The concentration of AuNPs prepared by sodium citrate and AuNPs prepared by r-CDs is 1.9×10−10 M, the concentration of r-CDs is 6.0×10−3 mg/mL, the concentration of H2O2 is 5.0×10−3 M, the concentration of TMB is 3.8×10−5 M, pH=4.0, the time between consecutive curves is 2min.

head.ce:title: Reduced state carbon dots as both reductant and stabilizer for the synthesis of gold nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analysis, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analysis
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The reduced state carbon dot (r-CD) is a new kind of carbon dot (CD) prepared by using sodium borohydride. Herein, we find that the r-CDs can directly reduce chloroauric acid to form gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) without adding other reducers or stabilizers. At the same time, we deduced that the hydroxyl groups (–OH) on the surfaces of r-CDs can act as both reducer and stabilizer for the synthesis of AuNPs. The AuNPs possess intrinsic catalytic activity, but compared with the AuNPs synthesized by citrate reduction, the AuNPs we prepared exhibit less excellent catalytic activity in the reduction of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) with sodium borohydride. Conversely, for catalyzing oxidation of the substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by H2O2, the AuNPs we prepared show much higher catalytic activity than the AuNPs synthesized by citrate reduction. This work may open up a new route for the applications of CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a new kind of fluorescent nanoparticles, were discovered serendipitously by researchers purifying single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004 [1]. Subsequently, this fluorescent nanoparticles which were dubbed CDs [2] by Sun et al. have attracted extensive attentions. Compared conventional organic dyes with semiconductor quantum dots, CDs possess superior properties such as low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and high stability against photobleaching [2–5]. Owing to above advantages, CDs were applied in many fields. CDs have been applied in biolabeling and bioimaging [2,6–8]. Chen and co-workers [9] and Qu and co-workers[10] fabricated photovoltaic devices and light-emitting diodes with CDs. Shi et al. [11] reported that CDs could catalyze the oxidization of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by H2O2, CDs also could be used as visible-light photocatalysts [12]. Zhou applied CDs in detecting mercury ion (Hg2+) [13]. However, as an excellent electron acceptor and electron donor [14], CDs has promising potential to be an oxidizing or reducing agent, which have seldom been reported.

Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been widely used in many areas, including catalysis [15,16], detection [17,18], therapy [19]. AuNPs were usually prepared by reducing chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) with a variety of reducing agents, such as citrate [20], sodium borohydride [21]. Alcohols were also often acted as reducing agents for synthesis of metal nanomaterials [22–24], but the reducing mechanism of different alcohols was different. For instance, gold, silver and palladium nanomaterials could be prepared by the reducing ability of the oxyethylene groups of poly(ethylene glycols) (PEG) [25]. The reducing mechanism of ethylene glycol (EG) was demonstrated that EG generated glycolaldehyde (GA) at the first time, then GA reduced Ag(I) to form silver nanoparticles [26]. Xia et al. [27,28] proposed that nanomaterials was prepared by using poly(vinyl pyrrolidone) which their ends were terminated with the hydroxyl group (–OH) and proved their reducing function derived from –OH.

Capping agent is another key factor of synthesis of nanomaterials and it can prevent nanomaterials from aggregating. Some capping agents such as thoils [20], oleic acid [29], hexadecyltrimethylammonium bromide [30] have been utilized to prepare metal and magnetic nanomaterials. But all of these are highly reactive chemicals which posing potential environmental and biological risks. As low-risk carbon nanomaterials, graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots have been applied in synthesis of metal nanomaterials acting as reducer or stabilizer [31,32].

Recently, our group synthesized reduced state carbon dots (r-CDs) successfully, which could be oxidized by some oxidants like KMnO4 [33]. Taking the reducibility of r-CDs and the oxidizability of HAuCl4 into account, AuNPs are synthesized successfully by simply mixing r-CDs with HAuCl4. We find that r-CDs act as both reducer to reduce HAuCl4 and stabilizer to prevent AuNPs from aggregating. The obtained AuNPs are monodispersed with an average diameter of ca. 16.4±3.8nm. Thus, this exploration may open a new scope of the applications of the CDs, as both reducer and stabilizer, in synthesis of metal nanomaterials.

HAuCl4 was purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Lamp black was obtained from Anhui Jixi Hukaiwen Ink Industry Co. (Xuancheng, China). Sodium borohydride was purchased from Tianjin Huan Wei Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Dialysis bags (molecular weight cut off=3500) were ordered from Shanghai Green Bird Science & Technology Development Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Unless otherwise stated, other reagents were of analytical grade and were used as received. Ultrapure water was prepared with a Milli-Q-Plus system (18.2MΩ) and was used throughout the experiments.

The r-CDs were prepared through two procedures. Firstly, CDs were synthesized from lampblack under HNO3 reflux [3,34]. Then the obtained CDs were reduced by NaBH4 [33]. Briefly, lampblack (0.5g) was added to a solution of HNO3 (5M, 150mL) and the mixture was heated under reflux at 140°C for 12h. And then, the solution were neutralized and purified by centrifugation and dialysis [3,34]. To prepare r-CDs, 0.5g of NaBH4 was added into solution of CDs (1×10−2 mg/mL, 150mL) under moderate stir [33]. At last, the excessive sodium borohydride was removed by thorough dialysis.

In a typical preparation, 6.0×10−2 or 1.2×10−1 mg/mL of r-CDs were mixed with 1.0×10−4, 2.0×10−4 or 4.0×10−4 M of HAuCl4, then the mixtures were heated for a certain period of time (1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12 and 24h) at different temperature (40, 60, 80 and 100°C). Finally, solutions were cooled to room temperature for characterizations. The citrate-capped AuNPs were synthesized according to the procedures described by Frens et al. [35].

1.0×10−2 M of NaCl was mixed with 1.9×10−10 M of aqueous solution of AuNPs for 4h incubation at room temperature, and the mixed solution was centrifuged for about 30min at 6000rpm. At last, the supernatant was kept at 4°C until use.

5.2×10−11 M of AuNPs prepared by r-CDs or AuNPs prepared by sodium citrate, 1.0×10−4 M of 4-NP were mixed with 7.5×10−3 M of NaBH4, the mixed solution were incubated for 60min at room temperature. And the reaction progress was checked by assessing a small portion of the reaction mixture at a regular time interval of 2 (before 30min) and 6min (after 30min).

According to experimental conditions in previous report [15], 1.9×10−10 M of AuNPs prepared by r-CDs or AuNPs prepared by sodium citrate or 6.0×10−3 mg/mL r-CDs and 3.8×10−5 M of TMB were mixed with 2.0×10−2 M of NaAc buffer (pH=4.0) and 5.0×10−3 M of H2O2, the mixed solution were incubated for 40min at 45°C. And the reaction progress was checked by assessing a small portion of the reaction mixture at a regular time interval of 2min.

The size and dispersivity of r-CDs and AuNPs were determined by a field emission transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Tec-nai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI, USA). The TEM samples were prepared by dropping solutions onto a 400-mesh carbon coated copper grids (SPI, USA) and dried in vacuum at 40°C. The absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded on a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and an F-4500 fluorospectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), respectively.

As shown in Fig. 1 , the r-CDs were spherical and monodisperse with an average diameter of 3.4±0.8nm (100 particles were measured). The r-CDs possessed a weak absorption peak at 280nm (Fig. S1). And the maximum excitation and emission of r-CDs were 280 and 440nm, respectively (Fig. S2). These results were in good agreement with the result reported previously by our group [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of AuNPs with the r-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
As demonstrated before [33], r-CDs possessed reducibility, it could reduce some oxidant, such as KMnO4, K2Cr2O7. Moreover, the standard electrode potentials (Eθ ) of HAuCl4 and KMnO4 are approximate
MnO
4
-
+ 8
H
+
+ 5
e
-
=
Mn
2 +
+ 4
H
2
O
, Eθ
=1.51V; Au3++3e− =Au, Eθ
=1.50V), which means that r-CDs may directly reduce HAuCl4 to form AuNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of r-CDs and HAuCl4 concentration on AuNPs synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: r-CDs with two concentrations were used in preparing AuNPs. Solution with higher concentration (1.2×10–1 mg/mL) was mixed with 1.0×10–4, 2.0×10–4 or 4.0×10–4 M HAuCl4. And a lower concentration (6.0×10–2 mg/mL) of r-CDs was mixed with 1.0×10–4, 2.0×10–4 M HAuCl4. And then, these mixtures incubated at 40°C for 24h, and the final product was denoted as C-Au-1, C-Au-2, C-Au-3, C-Au-4 and C-Au-5. As shown in Fig. S3, C-Au-1 is brown, C-Au-2 and C-Au-3 are black, and C-Au-5 is dark grey. And only C-Au-4 is wine-red, which is a characteristic color of AuNPs.

TEM was used to determine the size distribution of AuNPs. It was demonstrated that AuNPs were spherical. Under different incubation temperature, a slight difference of size can be observed (Fig. 4). The average diameter of AuNPs is 8.7±2.1 (40°C, 200 particles were measured), 16.4±3.8 (60°C, 200 particles), and 15.9±4.2nm (80°C, 200 particles). Although less obvious absorption peak could be found after incubating under 100°C, TEM revealed that AuNPs with an average diameter of 14.2±1.6nm (200 particles) were formed. Considering both the yield and size distribution of AuNPs, we chose 60°C in the further investigation.

We noticed that there are two unusual phenomena. Firstly, the yield of the AuNPs synthesized at 100°C is very low. It can be due to the oxidation of r-CDs by resolved oxygen at 100°C (Fig. S6). Secondly, the size of AuNPs did not increase monotonically with the temperature. To our best knowledge, there are two growth patterns during the growth of AuNPs. The first pathway is nucleation-growth route: in the first place, nuclei formed, subsequently, nuclei slowly growth. The second pathway consists of three overlapping steps: nucleation formation, random attachment to polycrystalline nanowires, and smoothing of the nanowires via intra-particle ripening to dots. At 40°C, though the second pathway also existed, the first pathway was predominant. At 60, 80 and 100°C, two growth patterns both existed, but the second pathway was more predominant with increasing temperature. So the size of AuNPs prepared at 60, 80 and 100°C is larger than that of AuNPs obtained at 40°C. At the same time, the rate of AuNPs growth increased with the increasing temperature, the size of AuNPs prepared at 100°C is smaller than that of AuNPs obtained at 60 and 80°C. UV–vis spectra and TEM image of the AuNPs synthesized at 60°C also demonstrated that the second pathway really existed (Fig. S7).

To obtain high yield of AuNPs, the incubating time was optimized. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), after 1h-incubation, the produced solution exhibited a wide and weak absorption peak from 500 to 700nm. However, an obvious absorption peak near 523nm appeared companied with the disappearance of absorption from 600 to 700nm after 2h-incubation, which is similar with what was observed by Peng [37]. It is indicated that the nearly spherical shape of the final products, which was also demonstrated by TEM image. The absorption of production at 523nm increased with the increasing incubation time but the wavelength did not undergo red shift or blue shift, and the full-width-at-half-maximum did not have obvious change as incubating time increases (Fig. 5(b)). As the incubating time reach to 12h, the absorption leveled off (Fig. 5(b)), which indicated the maximum yield of AuNPs. So we chose 12h as the optimal incubation time.

The surface of the CDs was rich in carbonyl groups (CO), while they changed into hydroxyl groups (–OH) after reducing by NaBH4 [33]. Corresponding to the change of the surface group, the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths changed [33]. After reducing by NaBH4, the maximum excitation wavelength of CDs changed from 470 to 280nm, at the same time, the maximum emission wavelength shifted from 520 to 440nm (Fig. S2). The change in fluorescence indicated the transformation of carbonyl groups (CO) into hydroxyl groups (–OH). As a reductive group, hydroxyl groups (–OH) onto r-CD surfaces can reduce HAuCl4.

Before reacting with HAuCl4, the intensity ratio ( I440 nm/I520 nm) of the r-CDs was 3.400 ( I440 nm and I520 nm was photoluminescence intensity under 280 and 470nm excitation). After reacting with HAuCl4 and separating the obtained AuNPs (Fig. S8), the ratio decreased to 2.217 (Fig. S9), which revealed that the characteristic emission intensity of the r-CDs decreased accompany with increasing characteristic emission intensity of the CDs. And thus we deduced that r-CDs reduced HAuCl4 to form AuNPs and turned to CDs after reaction.

Our deduction coincided with other reports [27,28]. It was reported that –OH terminated poly(vinyl pyrrolidone) could reduce Au(III) to Au(0) and proved their reducing ability derived from –OH. And the hydroxyl groups transformed into the carbonyl (CO) [27,28]. Hence, the reaction between r-CDs and HAuCl4 could be described as below (Eq. (1)):
(1)
r - CDs + Au ( III ) = CDs + Au ( 0 )

Capping agents are essential to prevent AuNPs from aggregating. At present, we find that only r-CDs were mixed with HAuCl4. And then obtained AuNPs kept stable for more than 6months and even didn’t show any color change or aggregation. Therefore, we believed that the r-CDs attached on the surface of AuNPs to protect AuNPs from aggregating. As reported by Liu et al. [39], hydroxyl groups could interact with AuNPs through hydrogen bonding. And the surface of r-CDs was rich in hydroxyl groups, hence the r-CDs could bind onto the surfaces of AuNPs. As a result, the AuNPs were stable and did not aggregate. But hydrogen bonding was a weak interaction and the r-CDs were not strong stabilizers, so the AuNPs would rapidly aggregate after the addition of 0.10M NaCl. The aggregation was harvested by 6000rpm centrifugation for 30min, and the supernatant had no absorption peak at ca. 520nm (Fig. S8). This result indicated that the r-CDs were weak capping agents to keep AuNPs from aggregating.

AuNPs can be used as catalyst in many reactions [16,40]. A model reaction, i.e., reduction of 4-NP by NaBH4 was chosen to evaluate the catalytic activity of AuNPs. Although the reaction is a thermodynamically feasible process involving E for 4-NP/4-AP=−0.76V and
H
3
BO
3
/
BH
4
-
= - 1.33
V
versus NHE, it is kinetically restricted in the absence of catalyst. The reduction process was monitored by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. The control experiment confirmed that the 4-NP could hardly react with NaBH4 in the absence of AuNPs (Fig. S10). We also found that r-CDs could not catalyze the reaction under the same conditions. However, in the presence of AuNPs prepared by r-CDs reduction, 4-NP could be reduced by NaBH4, as a result, the characteristic absorption of 4-NP at 400nm decreased with time while a new absorption peak of 4-AP near 295nm increased (Fig. S11(a)). At the same time, we applied the AuNPs prepared by sodium citrate to catalyze the same reaction in the same condition and obtained the similar results (Fig. S11(b)). However, using the AuNPs prepared by r-CDs as catalyst, approximate time of completion of reaction was a little longer (Fig. 6 ). Because the dosage of NaBH4 was excess, the concentration of
BH
4
-
was regarded as essentially constant throughout the reaction. Moreover, the UV–vis spectra also show an isosbestic point between two absorption bands, indicating that only two principal species, 4-NP and 4-AP, influence the reaction kinetics. Therefore, the catalytic rate constant (K) in this case could be evaluated by studying the pseudo-first-order kinetics with respect to 4-NP concentration [41,42]. The plots between ln(Ct /C0 ) (Ct and C0 are 4-NP concentrations at time t and 0, respectively) and reaction time (t) in the reduction reaction catalyzed by two kinds of AuNPs were obtained (Fig. 7 ), and K were calculated from the slopes of the linear sections. The AuNPs prepared by r-CDs (K =0.0585min−1) exhibited lower catalytic rates relative to AuNPs obtained by sodium citrate (K =0.108min−1). To our knowledge, catalytic reduction proceeded on the surface of the AuNPs, reactant molecule were adsorb on the surface of the AuNPs firstly [43]. Although the r-CDs and sodium citrate acted as stabilizer, nanoparticles (r-CDs) were larger than molecules (sodium citrate), so adsorption the surface of the AuNPs prepared by r-CDs became more difficult compared with AuNPs obtained by sodium citrate. Obviously, it is normal that the AuNPs prepared by r-CDs showed lower catalytic rates compared with AuNPs obtained by sodium citrate.

The TMB–H2O2 system was also applied as another model to estimate catalytic activity of some materials. It was well known that some AuNPs could catalytically oxidize TMB by H2O2 to produce a color reaction [15]. What surprise us is that the AuNPs prepared by r-CDs could catalyze the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 in NaAc buffer (pH=4.0), and produce the typical color reaction (Fig. S12(a)), the typical absorbance peak of oxidation products of TMB at 652nm increase with the increasing time. Though r-CDs show catalytic activity in this system (Fig. S12(b)), it is obvious that approximate completion time of reaction was longer than using AuNPs prepared by r-CDs as catalyst (Fig. 8 , Fig. S12(b)). At the same time, we found that the citrate-capped AuNPs (ca. 15nm) could hardly catalyze the reaction of TMB and H2O2 (Fig. 8, Fig. S12(c)). The difference in catalytic activity of these two kinds of AuNPs can be partly due to their different stability in buffer solution. It was found that the color of citrate-capped AuNPs turned blue immediately after the adding of NaAc buffer (pH=4.0) (Fig. S13). However, AuNPs prepared by r-CDs kept stable under the same condition (Fig. S13). The citrate-capped AuNPs aggregated and hence their catalytic activity was low.

We applied r-CDs to synthesize AuNPs successfully. It was confirmed that r-CDs acted as both reducer to reduce Au(III) and capping agent to keep AuNPs from aggregating. The reducibility of r-CDs may derive from the hydroxyl groups on surfaces of r-CDs. And the capping ability can be attributed to the hydrogen binding between AuNPs and hydroxyl groups. Although the AuNPs we prepared exhibit less excellent catalytic activity in the reduction of 4-NP with NaBH4, the AuNPs we prepared show higher catalytic activity than the AuNPs synthesized by citrate reduction for catalyzing oxidation of H2O2–TMB. Our findings open up a wide range of new potential applications for CDs in synthesis of metal nanomaterials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Scientific Program-Nanoscience and Nanotechnology (No. 2011CB933600), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21175110), the Natural Science Foundation Project of CQ CSTC (CSTC, 2010BB4004), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (XDJK2013A022).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2013.07.104.
